data.title: Smithfield: A Leader In Packaged Pork Products At A Deep Value Price
data.description:
Pork is the most consumed meat in the world, representing 36% of all meat consumed. It’s a staple of diets worldwide, particularly in China, Europe, and the US, and could grow in diet share thanks to a few tailwinds. For one, despite being classified as red meat, compared to beef, some studies have shown no harmful effects of pork consumption on cardiovascular risk factors. So pork consumption could increase as a result of consumers shifting to a healthier diet. Second, with beef (and poultry) prices breaking out of late, there is potential value in pork, which has not experienced such a dramatic price increase.

At 25% of the American diet share (compared to 50% for poultry and 25% for beef), there is room for the market share of pork in the U.S. to grow towards the global average of 36% as consumers reach for cheaper and/or healthier options at the grocery store.

Why Smithfield?

Smithfield (NASDAQ:SFD) is an old company that recently made its return to the public markets via a Nasdaq IPO on January 28th (though few noticed or took any interest). Back in 2013, Smithfield was taken private by WH Group for $4.7B. At the time, Smithfield was mainly known as a hog producer. But since going private, they have adopted several efficiency measures, started to reduce their reliance on hog raising, invested in manufacturing capacity, and sharpened their focus on higher-margin packaged meats.

Today’s Smithfield is a better company than 2013’s Smithfield, and you can invest in today’s Smithfield at a highly attractive valuation, lower than peers like Hormel (HRL) and Tyson Foods (TSN), and on par with 2013’s Smithfield. This low multiple does not take into account the transformation that has been occurring at the company over the last 10 years. Today’s Smithfield has a healthy balance sheet, providing flexibility to invest in production and strategic acquisitions, and pays a $1 dividend that is well covered by free cash flow and is likely to grow over time. Plus, insiders, including the CEO, chairman, and CFO, have been purchasing shares.

Smithfield’s Leading Brands

Smithfield is the largest pork processor in the U.S., and the second largest in terms of packaged meat sales by volume, with products across 25 categories. Their portfolio consists of leading brands like Smithfield, Eckrich, and Nathan’s Famous (64% of sales combined), and private label offerings across a range of price points (36% of sales). This balance gives them the opportunity to capture consumer demand in any economic environment (See Prospectus for more information). Smithfield brands are well-regarded—the company won the 2022 BrandSpark Most Trusted Award for pork products, and the 2025 International Foodservice Distributors Association Distributor’s Choice Award.

You can find Smithfield products in all of the top 10 national grocery retailers, including Walmart, Sam’s Club, Costco, Kroger, and Albertsons. And, if you like fast food, there’s a good chance you’ve eaten Smithfield products, because they serve 70% of the top 50 chains, including McDonald’s, Subway, and IHOP.

Products include bacon, sausage, hot dogs, deli, dry sausage (like pepperoni), and ham products, covering breakfast, lunch, and dinner – a unique feature of pork. Smithfield has a strong position in each of these subcategories, with #1 or #2 positions in bacon, sausages, deli meat, and smoked ham.

Source: January 29, 2025 Prospectus.

The Growth Opportunity

Smithfield is actively investing in its packaged meats business, improving efficiency and developing new, high-margin packaged products such as dried sausages and charcuterie. They are shifting away from traditional products, like holiday hams (low-margin hunks of meat). At the same time, they are shrinking the size of their hog production business, which is susceptible to huge swings in profit due to volatile commodity prices. For example, the company recently transferred part of its hog farming operations to Murphy Family Ventures LLC, and sold some farms in Utah and Missouri. These efforts will provide strong cost visibility, reduce the volatility of earnings, and boost overall margins. They are targeting a reduction to 30% internal hog production from around 50% today, which should still be sufficient to meet internal meat processing needs and retain the benefits of vertical integration. Overall margins have been steadily increasing as the company executes this strategy, but this transformation is still not reflected in the price of SFD shares.

Fortunately, the hog production segment of the business has been doing well of late. And, with ongoing favourable market conditions – strong pork prices combined with feed prices that have moderated significantly – this segment should continue to perform well in the near term.

Going forward, earnings growth will come from ongoing product mix improvements, volume growth, and innovation. For example, last year they expanded dry sausage capacity by 50M lbs to grow in this higher-margin product category, which increased 37% in sales between 2019 and 2024. This is a subcategory in which they have an 11% market share despite having the #2 position (see image above), so there’s plenty of room to take share. They’re also taking steps to increase sales of lunch meat through the Smithfield Prime Fresh premium brand.

The share of the packaged meats segment, already 58% of total sales, should continue to grow. This high-margin segment has grown profit at an impressive 9.8% CAGR over the past 10 years. With a roughly 20% overall market share, there remains space for Smithfield to continue to take share. In addition, as previously discussed, pork could take share from beef, as the cattle shortage in the U.S., which is keeping beef prices elevated, is not showing any signs of reversing. Pork consumption is also supported by the growing popularity in the U.S. of Asian and Mexican cuisines, which feature a lot of pork-based dishes. Referencing several forecasts from market research companies, Smithfield expects its pork product end markets to grow ~4% over the next 5 years.

With these tailwinds, the company is in a good position to continue to outperform, beat expectations, and raise guidance.

Valuation

Tyson Foods is probably the best comp for Smithfield. Even though the business has not done well recently, TSN trades at a forward earnings multiple of 14x, which is about average for the stock. Packaged food sales represent less than 20% of Tyson’s total sales ($2.5B of $13.9B total sales), with the rest coming from lower-margin meat sales.

Despite the fact that Smithfield has a higher proportion of sales coming from packaged meats, about 59%, SFD shares trade for just 10x forward earnings.

Pilgrim’s Pride (PPC) is optically cheap at 8.7x forward EPS, but the business is mostly focused on fresh meat sales, with prepared products accounting for 26% of revenues.

Hormel generates most of its revenues from packaged foods, and HRL stock trades at 17x forward earnings, which is on the low end of its historical range.

So, in light of these comps, what’s a reasonable target multiple for SFD? Certainly, multiple matching TSN’s would be reasonable, though you could also argue for a higher multiple, especially as Smithfield makes continued progress on improving its revenue mix towards higher-margin packaged meats.

At $2.39 consensus EPS for 2026 and a 14x multiple, equivalent to TSN, SFD shares would trade at $33.5 – a ~40% increase over today’s price.

Risks

Smithfield is a controlled company, with Hong Kong-based WH Group retaining about 87% ownership.

WH Group is led by 84-year-old Chair Wan Long. He has been involved with the company since the beginning, dropping out of high school and climbing the corporate ranks to the top, eventually spearheading an IPO in 1998 for the Shuanghui Group, which was renamed to WH Group in 2014.

Smithfield initially offered to sell 17.4M shares for $23-27 a share, but at the IPO, WH Group and Smithfield sold 13 million shares each at $20 a share, raising $522M. Mr. Long personally participated in the IPO, purchasing $40M of SFD stock and signaling that he believes the stock is undervalued.

A secondary offering by WH Group took place on September 8th, with 22.5M shares offered at $23.25 a share. Again, Mr. Long participated, buying an additional 1.8M shares. Smithfield executives, including the CFO, also participated in both the IPO and the secondary offering.

While the WH Group overhang scares off some investors, it’s encouraging to see insiders, including the WH Group chairman, absorb the shares. Plus, spinning off Smithfield seems to have been a rational decision and has been a success, so far, for WH Group. The price of SFD stock is up about 25% since the IPO, while WH Group stock is up around 50%.

Investors appear to be accepting the notion that a U.S.-market IPO for Smithfield is the best way to unlock value for both companies. WH Group has unlocked capital, while Smithfield has emerged with a clean balance sheet and better access to U.S. capital markets. Both companies now have separate boards of directors, and with Smithfield focused on the US and Mexico, and WH Group on China and Europe, the two groups no longer have to compete for capital under a single entity.

As a majority owner of SFD, with its ownership representing more than half of WH Group’s market cap, it simply makes no sense for WH Group to take actions that push the price of SFD stock down. So far, they are behaving like sound managers of the business. The secondary offering, which caused the stock price to fall almost 10%, has presented patient investors with a nice entry opportunity.

If WH Group does want to exit its stake completely, it will take a long time to do so. The good news is, with ample free cash flow and not much leverage, Smithfield should be able to steadily buy back shares from WH Group.

Smithfield is recovering from a terrible period in 2023, which featured rising feed costs and decreasing hog prices, causing operating profits to drop ~600M YoY. If this were to happen again, it would temporarily hurt the company’s profitability. However, recall that they are actively reducing the scale of their hog production business, so the impact should be lower than in the past.

Finally, a brief note on tariffs. Smithfield exports to over 30 countries, including China. Most of these exports are fresh pork products, and exports to China account for only about 2% of total sales. China has had tariffs on U.S. pork products in place for many years now, so while Trump’s trade war has made the situation worse, it’s not a new threat for Smithfield.

data.title: Cheniere Energy: Effects Of Power Of Siberia 2 On US LNG Exports Are Misunderstood
data.description:
Investment thesis: As the Power of Siberia 2 pipeline deal news surfaced, the most immediate reaction was to assume that this is a net negative for America's LNG exporters, such as Cheniere (NYSE:LNG). Putting aside the fact that it will be many years before the pipeline becomes operational, assuming that it will become operational at all, given that not every aspect of the deal is finalized yet, there is actually some potential upside for America's LNG exporters if this project does materialize. Among other things, it will help lock in America's dominance in the European market, giving it more pricing power, as Europe loses the ability to shift to alternatives if the price of natural gas is not competitive. Cheniere is currently a hold for me, but I am looking for a better entry point as a long-term investment, given that I see continued demand growth for LNG globally, even with the return of more Russian gas on the global markets.

Continued revenue & earnings growth, while maintaining a healthy profit margin, were in part achieved thanks to higher LNG prices.

For the first half of the year, Cheniere saw a 43% increase in net income to $1.98 billion and a 35% increase in revenues to $10.1 billion compared to the same period in 2024. Most of the increase came on the back of more favorable LNG prices, since its LNG sales volumes increased by a more modest rate of under 1% for the period. The improved financial performance is, therefore, arguably a reflection of LNG price volatility that can go either way. The fact that earnings increased at a faster pace than revenues suggests that some gains in profitability have been achieved along the way, which can be seen as perhaps the positive takeaway from these results. There are, of course, reasons to expect that LNG prices will not decline to last year's levels, which I shall discuss in the last section of the article.

Other results of note include the company's long-term debt situation, which stands at $22 billion, down just slightly from $22.5 billion at the start of the year. Interest expenses came in at $466 million in the first half of 2025. Interest expenses as a percent of revenues came in at 4.6%, which is in the danger zone from my perspective. I tend to worry about the financial health of companies that spend around 5% or higher of their revenues on servicing their debt.

The Power of Siberia 2 deal and Cheniere's exposure to any ramifications for the global market.

The news of the signed deal between Russia & China to build the Power of Siberia 2 pipeline was greeted as a globally significant event, with geopolitical, economic, and sectoral ramifications for the energy industry, given the large volumes involved of 50 Bcm/year. It remains to be seen if it actually goes ahead, with pricing disputes reportedly yet to be resolved. As of right now, it seems that Russia will have to sell the gas significantly below the price levels it would fetch on the European markets to clinch the final go-ahead for the project.

One of the prominent questions asked and speculations offered is the effect this will have on the longer-term prospects for US LNG exports.

As we can see, Europe, not Asia, is Cheniere's main market by far, since the all-out war in Ukraine started in 2022, which is also the case with most US LNG exporters. From this perspective, there is no direct impact on Cheniere's exports.

With the massive 50 Bcm/year volume, in addition to another 50 billion Bcm that is already flowing from Russia to China or is currently being planned, one can argue that this threatens global LNG demand, affecting the entire market as Russia's natural gas exports are set to return to levels we saw before the 2022 invasion of Ukraine once this pipeline will be in place. This may be true, but there is also continued global natural gas demand growth to consider, with demand rising by more than 50 Bcm/year currently. Some countries are seeing a decline in output capacity, such as Norway, for instance, where Europe's largest natural gas supplier is set to see output decline by 14 Bcm between 2024 and 2030.

The return of another 50 Bcms of Russian gas to the global market is a significant factor, with significant implications for the industry and individual companies. Once the pipeline deal is finalized and a start date for deliveries through the pipeline is provided, LNG companies should consider readjusting their plans for capacity expansion accordingly.

As we can see, the EIA is expecting flat US natural gas output going forward. In fact, the current quarter might see the highest average daily production at 107.2 Bcf/day, with production coming in slightly lower every quarter thereafter, at least until the end of 2026. Keeping this in mind, LNG companies might have other reasons aside from the China-Russia pipeline deal to consider whether they should invest in further LNG export capacities from this point forward.

Cheniere is engaged in expanding its Sabine Pass plant, with an intent to add as many as three more Trains to the six that are already operational. Its Corpus Cristi site is still in the middle of ramping up output, with the last Trains set to come online by the end of next year. If the EIA forecast for natural gas output to stagnate has merit, it is a sign of what is to come beyond the end of next year. Adding to it the uncertainty caused by the prospects of Russia regaining its pre-2022 place in the natural gas export markets, perhaps there are some valid questions about Cheniere's strategy of further increasing its LNG export capacity in the United States.

Looking at its expansion plans, versus its current P/E ratio at 15, currently on a forward basis, the company's continued growth trajectory, versus its P/E relative to other energy companies, is what makes it an interesting potential investment opportunity.

Compared with other energy companies, such as Shell (SHEL), the most dominant LNG player currently, which is trading at a forward P/E of just over 11, its P/E ratio is just slightly higher, even though it still has ample room to grow, while Shell is a mature, integrated energy company with few growth prospects. Of course, there are reasons to discount Cheniere's valuation, such as its high debt servicing costs, approaching 5% of revenues, whereas Shell spent 1.6% on servicing its debt in the first half of this year. There is also the dividend, which in the case of Shell, is almost 4%, compared with Cheniere's at less than 1%. At the same time, Cheniere's business volume could grow about 50% in the next few years based on existing expansion projects, with possibly more growth beyond, which makes it a growth stock that is currently trading more like a mature energy company.

Investment implications:

Cheniere's recent financial results have been impressive, but it has to be recognized that the main driver of those results has been the rise in LNG prices, especially on the European market.

It should be noted that natural gas prices in Europe have been trending lower in the past few months, despite the precarious inventory situation, relative to recent years; therefore, the current quarter might not be as impressive for Cheniere in terms of revenues and profits. A potential disappointment in its Q3 report might provide for a better buying opportunity.

The longer-term challenges for Cheniere do include the prospects of Russia and others, such as Iran, ramping up natural gas exports, but Europe, its main market currently and probably for the foreseeable future, is unlikely to buy much, if any gas from those countries, with plans in place to eliminate Russian energy from the European market. If anything, there is a positive aspect for Cheniere and its pricing power in the European market, because it eliminates the opportunity for Europe to pivot politically and return to importing significant amounts of cheaper pipeline gas from Russia, which used to be the price setter in the European market.

As for the issue of stagnating US natural gas output, perhaps making Cheniere's continued capacity expansion unwise, we have to take a closer look at how this would play out in terms of the US natural gas market. Stagnated output would lead to a rise in natural gas prices, which would then encourage more drilling, potentially providing more natural gas. It would also lead to lower domestic demand due to substitution effects. At some price point, utilities will switch to burning coal, as long as permitted by government regulations. In other words, Cheniere will probably have to pay a higher price for its natural gas purchases, but it will most likely be able to fully utilize its future export capacity.

Summing it up, I do see continued demand growth for LNG, and Cheniere will be able to utilize its investments in extra capacity by securing the necessary volumes of natural gas, even if it will have to pay more. Even with America's natural gas output stagnating, LNG exports can still increase as long as market forces are allowed to dictate flows. Cheniere's stock price is currently about 10% lower than its all-time high, and I do believe that it has some more potential downside on weaker Q3 results relative to the financial results we saw in the first half of the year. In the aftermath of its next quarterly report, there will probably be a better opportunity to invest, which is why I have it as a hold for now.

  • Risks to my thesis.

The main risk to my hold position is that the current situation with Europe's natural gas inventories can lead to a sizable LNG price surge this winter.

As we can see, inventories are below the five-year average and a sizable 14% below last year's levels, which also means that LNG prices are unlikely to retreat from current levels; in fact, the opposite might be the case. It would not take much of a weather-related event that causes an increase in natural gas demand in Europe to trigger a price spike. While there is a risk of missing out by waiting for Cheniere's Q3 results to come out, I also think that a European natural gas price spike is unlikely to happen by then. Market complacency has been evident, with little reaction to inventories being under the five-year average throughout 2025. I expect this complacency to continue, even if there will be some early signs of trouble for Europe's energy situation this winter.

  • Cheniere's current stock price is boosted by higher Y-O-Y LNG prices, which will not be the case for the rest of the year.

Aside from the debt-servicing situation that I find to be somewhat worrying, in terms of the proportion of revenues it takes to pay the yields, Cheniere has the financial profile of a company that is about to hit maturity, with a healthy financial situation overall, and good prospects for future performance. That performance is in large part underpinned by decent global LNG market prospects for the long term. This makes Cheniere a good long-term investment prospect; however, its financial performance metrics are likely to look somewhat worse after its Q3 results come out, which is why I intend to wait for now, even though there is some risk that waiting might mean an opportunity to invest will be missed.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Bond Brief: Spreads Are Tight, But Yields Are (Still) Alright
data.description:
By Gene Tannuzzo, CFA & Ed Al-Hussainy

It’s been quite a year so far for the bond market. While fundamentals remain strong, spreads are historically expensive, the labor market is in flux, and now the Federal Reserve (Fed) has started cutting rates. In this third quarter 2025 fixed-income update, we’ll look at how we’re adjusting portfolios to deliver value for investors in the current environment.

Fundamentals drown out political noise

Despite a turbulent start to 2025 and plenty of headlines, bond markets have shown impressive staying power, moving swiftly past the tariff turmoil following Liberation Day to refocus on fundamentals.

One of the most significant macroeconomic developments this quarter is the muted impact of tariffs on inflation. While tariffs initially sparked concern about rising prices, the data so far does not show seismic shocks to consumer prices. That’s largely because the U.S. economy is diversified and more domestically focused — roughly one-third of the personal consumption expenditure (PCE) basket consists of goods, and only one-fourth of those goods are imported. That means that just about 8% of the PCE basket is directly affected by tariffs. And with little in the way of excess (pent-up) demand, this insularity has helped keep inflationary pressure from trade disruption in check — for now.

At the same time, fiscal policy — in the form of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act — is also providing some welcome front-loaded, albeit limited, stimulus. These are all reasons we’re optimistic.

The caution in our optimism is the labor market, which remains the biggest conundrum as we head into year-end. Hiring has decelerated, but unemployment rates haven’t budged, reflecting a sharp slowdown in immigration. We're flying at a low altitude, so the margin of error for policymakers and investors is pretty small. The downside risk to the labor market has motivated the Fed to cut rates.

Moving from offense to defense in portfolios

These changes in fiscal and monetary policy have led to meaningful shifts in portfolio positioning heading into the third quarter. We took an offensive approach with credit during the April sell-off, when significant dislocations in bond markets and a widening of spreads created buying opportunities. We made significant adjustments to capitalize on these dislocations and have since taken profits on many of those positions. With credit no longer attractively priced, we're shifting to a defensive stance.

Historically tight credit spreads reflect continued corporate and economic strength. At the same time, yields across investment-grade and high-yield sectors remain compelling — investment-grade bonds are yielding around 5%, while high-yield bonds are closer to 7%. That’s a far cry from just a few years ago, when investment grade and high yield were yielding 1.5% and 3.5%, respectively.

What does this mean for bond investors? This is an environment that rewards careful credit selection and a focus on shorter duration, where investors can earn attractive coupons while managing risk. Within high yield, that means avoiding companies with unsustainable capital structures. In investment grade, it’s about relative value — with the corporate index and mortgage index both yielding 4.9%, the latter is implicitly guaranteed by the U.S. government.

Debt is financing the AI investment boom

Artificial intelligence (AI), once considered an equity story, now has the potential to drive significant issuance in fixed income. Technology and utility companies are borrowing to fund data center expansion and energy infrastructure for AI applications. If priced and structured correctly, these new opportunities could be compelling additions to portfolios.

Bonds remain an important diversifier

Bonds continue to prove their worth as portfolio diversifiers. The reason they matter for multi-asset portfolios is that they tend to move in the opposite direction to stocks, particularly in a significant risk drawdown. In a world where global equities have surged across regions and sectors, bonds are well positioned to outperform if equities turn. Moreover, amid concerns about fiscal policy and monetary policy credibility in the U.S., other developed market sovereign debt (such as Germany, France and the U.K.) can be a diversifier in a global bond portfolio. In short, diversifying your safe haven is a smart move in fixed income.

The bottom line

The bond market continues to move in response to policy shocks, but strong fundamentals have been — and will continue to be — a meaningful buffer. Investors should look past the headlines, focus on fundamentals and use bonds as strategic diversifiers. With attractive yields, tight spreads and falling rates, the playbook is clear: Stay selective and stay invested.

data.title: How To Gain Index Traction In Defense
data.description:
By Zoe Morrison, Senior Analyst, Equity Index Product

Geopolitical instability is rising, and there are widespread demands to increase defence spending, as well as a raft of recently announced national commitments to do so. Equity investors are paying close attention to this trend and share prices are moving as a result.

In this FTSE Russell Insight, we look at the indices from our range that best enable investors to express a market view on the burgeoning defence and aerospace sector.

Defence spending is on the rise

Last year saw a large acceleration in global defence spending, reflecting perceptions of growing geopolitical instability: according to the International Institute for Strategic Studies[1], global defence spending reached US$2.46trn in 2024, a big jump from US$2.24trn in the previous year.

This 7.4% inflation-adjusted increase outpaced the real-terms rises seen in 2023 and 2022 (6.5% and 3.5%, respectively).

Meanwhile, the Trump Administration has intensified the conversation with European NATO allies about defence funding, suggesting an increased allocation of national budgets to the military.

In January 2025, President Trump said that the minimum level of expenditure for NATO members should increase to 5% of gross domestic product (GDP)[2]. Currently, only Poland, Latvia, Estonia, Greece and the US spend more than 3% of their GDP on defence[3] - a significant shortfall by comparison with Trump’s stated goal.

Recent outperformance in Europe and the US

How can investors best express a sector-wide view on aerospace and defence stocks? The Aerospace and Defense sector falls within the Industrials Industry under the Industry Classification Benchmark (ICB), which is operated and maintained by FTSE Russell.

The Aerospace and Defense sector includes the manufacturers, assemblers and distributors of aircraft and aircraft parts that are used in commercial or private air transport. It also includes companies procuring components and equipment for the defence industry (including military aircraft, radar equipment and weapons).

However, the sector excludes manufacturers of communications satellites. These belong to the telecommunications equipment subsector (which lives within a separate ICB Industry, Telecommunications).

Recent outperformance in Europe and the US

The recent pressure on European governments to boost their military spending has led to significant gains in both European and US aerospace and defence stocks: reflecting the recent acceleration of defence spending on both sides of the Atlantic Ocean, both the FTSE Europe All Cap Aerospace and Defense index and the Russell 3000 Aerospace and Defense index have delivered healthy outperformance vis-à-vis their parent indices during the last one, three and five years.

Taking a view on defense

Amidst rising security needs, growing political uncertainty and the possibility of long-term government contracts, the Aerospace and Defense sector is an area of focus for equity market participants. Companies within the sector continue to drive technological innovation and its performance attributes support portfolio diversification. data.title: CIO Notebook: Fed Makes A Move, Adds Fuel For Future Cuts
data.description:
By Shannon L. Saccocia, CFA

The decision was primarily driven by the committee judging that slower job gains and higher unemployment outweighed inflation concerns, prompting a more accommodative policy stance.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell’s speech at Jackson Hole, coupled with continued deceleration in the U.S. labor market over the last several weeks, significantly raised the probability of a Fed cut coming into the meeting.

Therefore, the Federal Open Market Committee’s (“FOMC”) decision to cut the fed funds target rate by 25 basis points to 4.0% to 4.25% was no real surprise, and one could argue that the bigger question was how many FOMC voters would have preferred more.

The answer it turns out was just one – the newly sworn-in Stephen Miran – while both Michelle Bowman and Christopher Waller, who had dissented in favor of a cut in July, went with the majority in the September meeting.

The statement released following the meeting acknowledged the key driver of the decision: “the shift in the balance of risks.” While acknowledging that “inflation has moved up and remains somewhat elevated,” it was the slower job gains, the move higher in unemployment, and, perhaps most importantly, the judgment that “downside risks to employment have risen” that spurred the FOMC into action.

Changes in the dot plot reflect not only Wednesday’s interest cut but also a more accelerated pace moving through 2028. For this year, the dot plot reflects two additional cuts from here, translating to 3.6% by the end of 2025; estimates for the fed funds rate at the end of 2026 and 2027 are 3.4% and 3.1%, respectively.

While the expectations for 2025 appear firmly centered around 3.6% with only one meaningful outlier, there is much wider dispersion as one looks out to 2026 and 2027.

The implication is that there is still significant uncertainty in the path for both the labor market and inflation, and it will likely be a continued push and pull as each side of the inflation-employment tug of war fights for prominence in the narrative over the next several months.

Interestingly, the quarterly update of economic projections painted a rosier view than the projections released in June. The Fed is now projecting that the US economy will grow by +1.6% in 2025 and +1.8% in 2026, versus June’s projections of +1.4% and +1.6%, respectively.

Core PCE is expected to close this year at +3.1% – in line with June’s release – and +2.6% in 2026, slightly higher than last quarter. Perhaps most importantly, the Fed maintained its estimate for the unemployment rate to close the year at 4.5% and to fall modestly in 2026 to 4.4% – hardly cause for significant concern.

During the press conference, Fed Chair Powell emphasized the importance of the dual mandate and admitted that the potential risk of a more protracted deceleration in the U.S. labor market was the rationale for the move towards a more accommodative stance.

When attempting to square the increase in growth expectations with the rate cut, Powell admitted that the move could be described as a “risk management” cut, which appeared to rankle equity investors who had pushed prices higher immediately following the release of the Fed’s statement.

In terms of inflation, Powell continues to lean into the thesis that the goods inflation evident in recent prints is likely to be one-time in nature, and with the low hiring, low firing labor dynamic, wage inflation should remain subdued.

Finally, any attempts to engage Powell on the political pressure the Fed is under, the potential conflict represented by Stephen Miran’s dual employment, or a potential probe of the Fed as teased by Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent were swiftly and effectively dispensed with by the Chair with essentially no comment.

In short, Wednesday’s announcement was as predicted but perhaps shines a bit of a brighter light on what to expect as we move closer to 2026. Policy uncertainty remains an overhang, and while there are some FOMC voters that appear ready and willing to lean in hard on the labor market, the lack of any real momentum behind a 50 basis point cut supports the view that the Fed remains both independent and cognizant of the challenges another round of structural inflation could bring.

Markets were mixed on the news, with U.S. small cap and cyclical equities higher following the press conference; volatility and the NASDAQ were both lower in late session trading. Short yields moved lower, while longer maturities were incrementally higher – likely reflecting longer-term inflation and growth expectations.

data.title: Shake Shack: Valuation, Fundamentals Promise Upside, But Technicals Warrant Caution
data.description:
Three months after my second analysis, Shake Shack Inc. (NYSE:SHAK) is now back to reality. After hitting its new all-time high, SHAK went into a pullback below $100. I must say I was a bit too early when I took profits at $130, but I still think I made the right decision. After all, SHAK is already 24% lower than my publication price. This is where things get more exciting because its valuation is already quite far below my target price or TP. Fundamentals support my view considering its strong performance amid inflationary headwinds. Technicals are still weak as bearish signals persist, but buying opportunities are emerging again.

Valuation: SHAK Makes Sense Again

Shake Shack increased higher and faster than it should in my last coverage. While its performance remained robust, I felt that the market was too optimistic despite macroeconomic uncertainty. I understand that fast-casual diners have their own natural insulation against inflation.

Now, SHAK trades at $99, which is already far below my previous target price of $110. And I must say I'm keeping an eye on it again as it becomes cheap again. With that, I updated my valuation of its price relative to its sales per share and included cash per share. If you can remember, sales per share and the stock price are 80% correlated with only a 3% p-value in my regression analysis. This shows the consistency in their direct relationship and that the pattern did not happen by chance. I believe using the same metric makes sense.

Using P/S, the stock now trades at 3.00x its sales per share, which is below the five-year average of 3.45x. It is also in the mid-range if we rank its valuation from cheapest to most expensive since 2020. This makes it reasonable at this point. If we use the average to derive the fair value, the TP will be $113.71. Likewise, P/CPS shows price sensitivity to cash per share. The price increased and decreased along with it in the past five years. In addition, the stock trades at 11.83x its cash per share versus the five-year average of 12.35x. Using this, the TP is $103.40.

To prove it, I also did a regression analysis on CPS and price with 26 observations. True enough, their correlation is also high at 84%, while the p-value is only 0.02 or 0.2%. This shows a high probability that the price will move along with the CPS since their relationship did not happen by chance. Like what I did to sales per share in my previous article, regression analysis uses historical data. However, it gives us a clue about the price direction relative to these financial metrics. If sales per share and/or cash per share increase, there is at least an 80% probability that the price will follow. Hence, both ratios suggest that the recent pullback was justified but a bit too much.

I Like Shake Shack

As a restaurant, I like the quality of its food. I don't in other countries, but I've already tried eating in three different branches here in the Philippines, and so far, their food quality is consistent. Its Shack Burge, SmokeShack, Cheese Fries, and Chocolate Milkshake are top tier for me.

Food taste aside, I like SHAK for its strategic business model. As a fast-casual diner, it still has some level of competition with quick-service restaurants. But diners like Shake Shack have some insulation against inflation since they capitalize mainly on value propositions rather than cheaper pricing. True enough, it still benefits from stubborn inflation at 2.9%, which may be exacerbated by new tariffs. Why? Their price is strategically higher than fast-food restaurants but not so high as to cause a wide disparity or differentiation. This provides some flexibility to capture more demand without sacrificing profits.

With respect to location, I noticed that Shake Shack does not open in any mall. I can only see it in our national airport, malls typically flocked by middle-income and high-income households, and shops within central business districts. For instance, there is one in the Bonifacio Global City or BGC, which is the center of many large international companies. There is also one in SM Mall Of Asia, the largest mall in the Philippines, which is also near five-star hotels, high-end condominiums, and international businesses. As a disclaimer, I'm speaking from my perspective in the PH setting. But I believe this shows that SHAK remains prudent and cautious with franchising requirements and documentation, such as site selections and market studies, including foot traffic, competition, and overall demand. It does not approve or permit franchising just for the sake of international expansion.

Considering all this information, it's not surprising that most, if not all, Shake Shack restaurants remained strong as their systemwide sales rose by 13.7% YoY to $549.9M. With 610 restaurants in operation, sales per restaurant reached $901,475 on average. This was still much better than in Q2 2024, with $483.7M in systemwide sales and $884,278 in sales per restaurant. This was also higher than in my previous coverage, which had $489.4M in systemwide sales and $830,900 in average restaurant sales. This shows a combination of increasing demand and improved productivity of each Shack unit amid its expansion.

Q2 2025 Q1 2025 Q2 2024
Systemwide Sales $549.9M $489.4M $483.7M
Restaurant Count 610 589 547
Average Sales Per Restaurant $901,475 $830,900 $830,900

On a company level, SHAK also performed well as its operating revenue reached $356.5M, up by 12.6% YoY from $316.5M. It should also not surprise us because both revenue components rose as both company-operated and franchised restaurants improved on a YoY basis. It is also good to see a slight increase in the percentage of licensing revenues to the total amount from 3.5% to 3.7%. I think this will be helpful for SHAK since increasing franchisees for international expansion and/or brand recognition is cheaper and less cost-sensitive than building owned restaurants. With that, its costs and expenses remained manageable relative to revenues. Its operating income doubled, and margin increased from 3% to 6% YoY.

Lastly, I like SHAK's robust liquidity. As a restaurant business with a high concentration of owned restaurants versus franchisees, SHAK is impressive. Its cash level has increased in the past 11 quarters. This is more than enough to cover all current liabilities at once even if it doesn't have to. Its debt is also stable and lower than cash.

We can look at its cash flow statement to see the adequate operating cash inflow. In Q2 2025, it had an FCF of $27M, which means that it converted 5% of sales into free cash. This is also much higher than in Q2 2024 and in my previous coverage. FCF is also enough to cover debt repayments and stock repurchases. This means that its profits or cash inflows can sustain its operations. As you can see, SHAK has not issued debt in the past 11 quarters. Hence, it stays very liquid with a high capital buffer.

Technicals

SHAK's technicals are still weak as bearish signals persist. The stock price is still decreasing below the 50-day and 200-day SMAs, showing that selling trends remain strong. The 50-day SMA is still trending downward and might cross below the 200-day SMA. This means that the downtrend still holds, and market sentiments remain weak.

Meanwhile, MACD and RSI reflect the price trend, so we cannot detect any bullish divergence yet. As we can see, the MACD line is still in negative territory, which means that selling volume still outpaces buying volume. The only consolation is that the MACD line is increasing again above the signal line, while the histogram just returned to positive territory. These two instances show that buying volume is starting to pick up its momentum again. I think that's normal after its sharp dip. The recent low has attracted re-entries and new buyers. But again, selling volume is still higher than buying volume, so the trend is still weak today. Its RSI explains the sharp dip, which could be due to overbuying at its new all-time high. Rebound attempts are due to the recent overselling, so many buyers are seeing the dip as new entry opportunities. After all, the stock trades at a discount. It is still near the oversold region, so buying room is still very wide.

Lastly, the stock still respects the Fibonacci Retracement. Now, the price is moving within the 0.382-0.50 points. This is also the first time the price has attempted to consolidate after the continued downtrend. While the trend remains bearish, there is still a probability that the price will increase to 0.50-0.618, which is in line with its TP.

Conclusion

Shake Shack, Inc.'s valuation has become realistic and reasonable again after a sharp dip from its new all-time high. The upside potential is supported by its solid performance, driven by its strategic business model. Its balance sheet also remains well-positioned, which assures us of its capacity to sustain its expanding operating capacity with its robust cash inflows and prudent debt management. The challenge now is its technicals that still present bearish signals despite new buying opportunities. I'm upgrading my rating on this stock to buy but advising investors to remain cautious as technicals still defy fundamentals.

data.title: Energy's Time To Shine: Why FENY Could Be The Biggest Winner Of Inflation, Rate Cuts, And Deficit Spending
data.description:
U.S. inflation has been above the 2% target for years and in the last three months has moved further away from it. Nevertheless, the Federal Reserve is planning to ease monetary policy through rate cuts. At the same time, spending from Washington is on the rise and fiscal deficits will continue to expand. Historically, this kind of environment has been highly supportive of real assets and particularly the energy sector, including the Fidelity MSCI Energy Index ETF (NYSEARCA:FENY).

Here's why I think the energy sector is well positioned to outperform: it has inflation-hedging characteristics, underinvestment in new capacity lasted for years, companies reward shareholders through record buybacks, and valuations are low relative to the rest of the market.

Macro Tailwinds

Historically, energy has been one of the few sectors that directly benefit from inflation. As energy demand is relatively inelastic in the short to medium term — people still drive to work, trucks still need to deliver groceries to supermarkets — higher oil and gas prices flow almost immediately into producer revenues. We saw this in 2022 when oil surged above $100 a barrel after Russia's invasion of Ukraine. Global demand stayed strong, and there was only a small reduction in demand growth compared with pre-war expectations.

Right now, inflation (PCE index) is at 2.88%, and more importantly, it is drifting further from the target, yet the Fed is moving its focus to the labour market and is leaning toward a new easing cycle. That kind of monetary policy tends to push real yields lower, which can add further pressure to the dollar's weakness and increase demand for hard commodities. Historically, weak dollar boosted crude prices, as globally oil is traded in dollars.

For example, after the Dot-com bubble burst, the Fed aggressively cut the Federal funds rate, the U.S. annual budgets moved from surpluses to deficits, and inflation moved from 1% in early 2022 to just under 5% in 2005. During the same period, the Dollar index dropped from around 115 to roughly 90, and crude oil prices tripled from $20 to $60.

In 2024, the budget deficit was 6.4%, while in this year despite the tariff revenue it should increase to around 6.55%. I wrote about this topic in my previous article, but in a nutshell, the effects of the One Big Beautiful Bill are still not reflected in the current deficit. However, in the next decade, the Bill is expected to increase deficits by $3.4 trillion, strongly pushing the debt/GDP ratio even higher from the current 119%.

Massive government spending, no matter whether on defense, infrastructure, or social programs, raises energy demand. Trucks move more goods, plants work overtime, data centers need more power. Every dollar of stimulus goes through the economy, raising energy consumption. Therefore, the energy demand looks well supported even in a slower macro environment.

Tight Supply Dynamics

The supply side is where things really get interesting, as oil production facilities take years to build, while for a long time the global oil and gas companies underinvested. This raises the odds that an increase in demand could push prices higher.

Crude oil (West Texas Intermediate) has underperformed the broader commodity market for years. For example, in the last 4 years, the Bloomberg commodity index is up around 6%, while in the same period, crude oil dropped 13%. The prices spiked after Russia invaded Ukraine, but then steadily drifted lower, and at those levels, big oil and gas companies mostly preferred to return cash to shareholders and abstain from significant capital expenditures (CapEx).

Following the oil price collapse of 2014-2016, when prices dropped from around $100 to approximately $40, the chart below shows global upstream investment peaked and then dropped sharply. According to the International Energy Forum (IEF), annual upstream oil and gas capital expenditures fell by nearly half from 2014 levels and remain below the investment required to meet even modest demand growth.

According to the International Energy Agency (IEA), refinery investments in 2025 are set to fall to their lowest level in at least a decade. Asia and the Middle East will account for around 75% of global investments this year, while the share of advanced economies will drop to just 10% in 2025 from 34% in 2015. It's quite worrying how little advanced economies invest in refinery capacities, increasing their dependency on emerging economies, particularly China, India, and the Middle East.

War in Ukraine, Middle East tensions, and OPEC+ production controls add further risks to the supply side. ESG pressures and regulatory hurdles have deterred long-lead investment projects, such as deepwater drilling or refinery investments, as they are a major source of emissions. Additionally, existing oil fields throughout the world decline naturally over time, typically at 4–6% annually. Therefore, without sufficient reinvestment, global supply is likely to remain tight relative to demand.

Shareholder Returns

Major oil companies like ExxonMobil, ConocoPhillips, and Chevron — accounting for around 45% of FENY holdings — have deliberately prioritized dividends and buybacks over CapEx. All three companies are generating significant free cash flows, which are mostly used for dividend growth and multi-billion-dollar buyback programs. This means supply cannot expand quickly, reinforcing the bullish thesis for existing producers.

As the three charts below show, ten years ago buybacks were almost irrelevant, and even when dividends were included, capital flows to shareholders were much smaller than CapEx. Today, the picture is completely different: Exxon and Chevron allocate about 60% of capital to buybacks and dividends, while ConocoPhillips allocates 44%.

Low Valuations Vs. Broader Market

Out of eleven S&P 500 sectors, energy has the cheapest valuation ratios. It has the lowest trailing 12-month price-to-earnings, price-to-cash flow, and price-to-book ratios. Additionally, its trailing 12-month dividend yield of 3.41% is the highest of all sectors.

The same holds true for valuations of Fidelity MSCI Energy ETF (FENY), which are in line with the S&P 500 energy sector averages.

Despite generating record profits in recent years, the energy sector trades at a discount relative to the rest of the market, and energy equities have not enjoyed the same multiple expansion as the broad market. If oil prices rise, this valuation gap could provide asymmetric upside, as earnings would accelerate from already discounted multiples.

Risks to Monitor

A global economic slowdown poses a risk, as a severe recession could weigh on oil demand and negatively impact revenues and profits. There are also several policy risks, such as accelerated green transition initiatives, windfall taxes, or restrictive regulations. All these risks could reduce revenues and/or increase costs and consequently affect profitability.

Another risk is a rebound in U.S. shale drilling, which could soften the underinvestment thesis by boosting supply and limiting oil price appreciation.

Conclusion

Considering the macro backdrop of inflation running above target, the Fed cutting rates, and expanding fiscal deficits coupled with relatively low energy valuations, the energy sector looks well positioned to outperform. Historically, the sector offered a natural hedge against inflation and should also benefit from dollar weakness and reflationary demand. Additionally, there is a structural tailwind of supply scarcity from years of underinvestment.

data.title: Everspin Technologies: Is More Expensive Than It Looks At First
data.description:
Everspin Technologies (NASDAQ:MRAM), the leading provider of magnetoresistive RAM, or MRAM, did what it could to give the stock a lift, which included, among other things, a return to growth by reporting its first YoY increase in quarterly revenue after five consecutive contractions in its most recent quarterly report, yet the stock found it difficult to get past what appears to be resistance in the charts. That is until the last couple of days after the stock achieved what proved to be elusive for over a year. However, while the breakout might be viewed as bullish and a reason to be long on Everspin, there is still an argument to be made against long Everspin, depending on how earnings are defined. Why will it be covered next.

Everspin overcomes stiff resistance

A previous article from last June was written with the stock in the midst of a furious rally that made it possible for Everspin to gain over 50% in market cap. Everspin had all the momentum on its side, which made it tempting to jump in to take advantage of the rally, but the article nonetheless questioned the sustainability of the rally in the stock, especially as the rally came on the heels of a big selloff. Without the latter, the former would likely not have happened. Accordingly, I refrained from joining in by rating Everspin a hold in the article.

The chart above shows how the stock had trouble maintaining the rally until the last couple of days. Notice how the stock went sideways after reaching an intraday and 52-week high of $7.08 on July 16., only to close the day lower at $6.92, or below $7.00, which foreshadowed the inability of Everspin to move higher than $7.00 in the weeks and months that followed.

There were numerous instances in the last few months of the stock coming close to $7.00, and brief forays above $7.00 on an intraday basis. From July 10 to July 12, for instance, the stocks reached intraday highs of $6.96, $6.99, and $6.97, only to close lower by the end of the day. These struggles with the $7.00 price level go back quite some time and not just the last several months. Notice how in the chart above, for instance, Everspin peaked at exactly $7.00 in November 2024 before the stock turned lower.

This changed with the stock breaking out and closing above the $7.00 level in the last couple of days. Notice how the stock quickly spiked higher on elevated volume to a new 52-week high of $8.11, or well above $7.00, which suggests that the breakout caused buyers to jump in after resistance was breached.

Everspin can look reasonably priced, but also expensive, depending on what one considers real earnings

However, while the recent breakout after being held back by resistance for a long time is likely to be seen as bullish by some, there is nonetheless an argument to be made against joining the fray. A look at earnings results from Everspin shows how an argument can be made that Everspin is actually a lot more expensive than it looks at first.

The bulls were likely emboldened to bet on Everspin since the most recent, or Q2 FY2025, report included a return to growth at Everspin, something that greatly benefited the bottom line after dealing with contraction for several quarters. Revenue increased by 24.5% YoY to $13.2M in Q2 FY2025, which resulted in a GAAP loss of $0.7M, or $0.03 per share, up from a loss of $2.5M, or $0.12 per share, in Q2 FY2024, and a non-GAAP profit of $0.7M, or $0.03 a share, much better than the loss of $0.6M, or $0.03 a share, one year ago.

The main difference between the GAAP and non-GAAP numbers is the latter’s exclusion of stock-based compensation expense, which amounted to $1.4M in Q2 FY2025. Everspin finished the quarter with cash and cash equivalents of $44.96M, partially offset by $4.0M of total debt. Guidance calls for growth to continue with revenue of $13.5-14.5M in Q3 FY2025, up from revenue of $12.1M in Q3 FY2024, which is forecast to result in non-GAAP income of $0.02-0.07 a share.

Everspin earned $0.07 in terms of GAAP but $0.35 in terms of non-GAAP in the past 12 months. This means Everspin trades at a P/E ratio of 112.3x in terms of the former, but a more palatable 22.5x in terms of the latter, with the stock last closing at $7.86. Remember, though, that the use of the latter requires leaving out stock compensation expense when determining earnings.

Furthermore, it’s important to mention that the improvement in the bottom line got a major boost from the DoD contract Everspin was awarded in August 2024. This added $0.8M of other income in Q2 FY2025, which means that all of the non-GAAP net income of $0.7M can basically be attributed to government cash. Of the $14.6M awarded last year, Everspin has received $7.4M to date.

Leave out this cash infusion, and Everspin lost money instead of earning $0.07 in terms of GAAP TTM, and TTM non-GAAP net income would be more like around $0.6M or $0.03 a share instead of the reported $0.35. If not for the DoD contract awarded to Everspin, or more specifically the cash Everspin got from the government, and if Everspin had to rely solely on what the business itself generated in terms of income, Everspin would be trading at a P/E of about 262x, which is very different from a P/E in the low twenties like 22.5x.

It's also worth pointing out that while revenue grew by 24.5% YoY in the last quarter, revenue has been flattish in the last three quarters at around the $13M quarterly run rate. If we were to extrapolate this flattish growth out into the future, then FY2026 revenue could end up at about $55M, which is not much better than the current TTM revenue of $51.6M, but pretty consistent with what Everspin has done over the last five years with a flattish top line.

But while the top line may be higher, the bottom line is likely to be lower because, unlike FY2025, FY2026 will only partially benefit from the DoD award because most of it will have been spent by then. The DoD has not revealed the schedule of disbursements, and it is possible to spread it out over additional quarters, but about half or $7.4M has already been disbursed to Everspin.

If we then assume this number goes up to about $10M by the time FY2025 is over, then about $4.6M of the original $14.6M will be available at the start of FY2026. Add another $1M of estimated income from the business itself, and this suggests FY2026 non-GAAP net income of about $5.6M, or $0.25 a share, less than current TTM earnings, but keep in mind that most of it is from the DoD award, and it leaves out stock compensation expense. It also assumes Everspin does not receive additional financial assistance from the government, which is not impossible.

Why the government assistance makes a huge difference for Everspin

Paying a very high multiple can still be worth it if the stock grows earnings fast enough to bring the multiple down to more common levels. Yet here too Everspin arguably falls short because it requires making some compromises. For instance, according to the methodology espoused by Peter Lynch, a P/E ratio of 22.5x means that Everspin needs to grow earnings at a CAGR of 22.5% in the next 3-5 years to be deemed fairly valued relative to growth.

This is actually much more than what Everspin was able to achieve in the past five years, which saw earnings contract during these years, but a CAGR of 22.5% in the next 3-5 years is theoretically possible, especially if the current U.S. government, which is big on domestic manufacturing of semiconductors, provides more financial help.

However, if one were to leave out the government cash Everspin got, which would give Everspin a P/E of 262x as shown earlier, then according to the same methodology, Everspin would need to grow earnings at a CAGR of 262% in the next 3-5 years, which looks very difficult to achieve. Keep in mind that this does not even account for stock compensation expense, which would make the task at hand even more difficult.

In other words, the case for long Everspin requires making several concessions in terms of what Everspin is really earning to come up with a CAGR for earnings that could conceivably be achieved. You would need to exclude stock compensation expense, and you would need to ignore the fact that the cash infusion from the government is only temporarily and not permanent. This is unlikely to be acceptable to everyone.

Is MRAM keeping up with other forms of non-volatile memory?

Everspin specializes in MRAM, a type of non-volatile memory, but there are other types of non-volatile memory on the market that could, on paper, take the place of MRAM. For instance, Everspin has been targeting the automotive market with, for instance, products that qualify with the AEC-Q100 automotive standard.

Yet reception has been rather tepid for Everspin. In comparison, a competing option like ReRAM seems to have more momentum on its side. For instance, ReRAM has been adopted by a major player like ON Semiconductor (ON), a major supplier of automotive chips, which recently licensed related IP from Weebit Nano. It is possible that MRAM will do better in other markets, like in the market for LEO satellites, but if the automotive market, which is at trillions one of the biggest markets around, is indicative of how MRAM will fare in other markets, then Everspin has its work cut out.

Another example is InnoStar Semiconductor, a leading ReRAM supplier in China, which has received backing from big players like Alibaba (BABA) and ByteDance. While both MRAM and ReRAM are by no means the only types of non-volatile memory, they both seek to achieve the same, which is the ability to store and retain data even when power is cut off, like flash NAND memory, but with support for much higher transfer speeds, close to that of DRAM.

In essence, both seek to combine the best of flash memory and DRAM into a single solution, instead of the traditional approach of using separate solutions, but they differ in their approach. MRAM and ReRAM have their respective strengths and weaknesses with the former, for instance, much longer around than other competitors like ReRAM, which means it is the more mature solution, something to take into account in, for instance, mission critical applications.

On the other hand, unlike magnetic MRAM, which is completely different compared to regular flash memory, ReRAM is more similar, though differences remain. This means ReRAM is easier for existing flash manufacturers to incorporate with existing production facilities, which suggests ReRAM will have a cost advantage over the more difficult and harder to scale MRAM.

There is much more to mention, but the big advantage of ReRAM at this time seems to be that it is more widely available from multiple vendors, including foundries. In contrast, there seems to be less interest in entering the market for MRAM, which could be a sign of which form of non-volatile memory companies think has the better future, at least at this time.

Investor takeaways

Everspin has found it difficult to get companies to adopt MRAM over the years, which is part of the reason why sales contracted for five consecutive quarters recently. This changed in the Q2 FY2025 report, which recorded a YoY increase in revenue, but it is worth mentioning that on a sequential basis, revenue has been flat for three straight quarters. Q2 FY2025 revenue of $13.2M represents an increase of 24.5% YoY, but it also represents a revenue level that Everspin reached as early as in 2018, or seven years ago. Growth at Everspin has had its ups and downs.

The rather flattish growth in the top line is a major reason why earnings have shrunk in recent years to the point that Everspin recorded net losses. Everspin was able to improve in this regard more recently, but it is important to note that almost all of the earnings are due to government assistance, even when using non-GAAP and its less strict definition of earnings. While Everspin is still entitled to about half of what it was awarded by the government last year, which means the next several quarters will continue to benefit from other income, the effect on the income statement is not permanent, but will eventually run out.

This issue is important because it impacts how Everspin should be valued. If the cash infusions are not left out, and stock compensation is, then the stock trades with a P/E of 22.5x, using non-GAAP, which suggests Everspin could be worth buying since it could conceivably grow earnings at a CAGR in the 20% range to be deemed fairly valued, if not undervalued, relative to growth.

On the other hand, if the government money is left out, then Everspin does not trade in the low twenties, but well into the triple digits, which suggests Everspin is likely overvalued and not worth buying because it is very unlikely to grow earnings at a corresponding pace in the triple digits. It is even worse if one takes stock compensation expense into account, or GAAP numbers, which would further reduce whatever earnings Everspin has and in turn further increase the rate at which earnings need to grow in the coming years to justify the current stock price.

I am therefore neutral on Everspin with the above in mind. It is possible for the stock to move higher, if only because of momentum, but even if this were to happen, it would not change the thesis laid out in this article as an increase in the stock price would only increase the multiples assigned to the stock, which, depending on how one defines earnings, is arguably already too high in the triple digits.

While some may have no issue with government cash greatly increasing earnings, I am of the opinion that the government money gives an inflated picture of how well Everspin is doing, especially as it is only temporarily, and that a P/E in the triple digits and not the low twenties is the more accurate representation of Everspin’s ability to generate sustainable earnings. This means standing pat on Everspin.

data.title: Expensify: Tough To Argue With Solid Cash Flow
data.description:
With the market soaring to new heights, we as investors should recognize openly that the vast majority of the year's gains have been driven by disproportionate rallies in large-cap, momentum-driven tech stocks. With large-cap multiples at historic highs, those of us who are looking to protect ourselves from potential downside should be emphasizing rotating toward more value-oriented small and mid-cap stocks.

Expensify (NASDAQ:EXFY) is a great choice in this regard. The T&E software and corporate spending company has seen a precipitous ~50% decline in its share price since the start of the year, despite measurable progress in its go-to-market initiatives and rising cash flow. In my view, it's a great time for investors to reassess the bull case in this name.

I last wrote a "Buy" rating on Expensify in July, when the stock was trading closer to $2.50. Since then, Expensify shares have continued to showcase weakness, despite a raise to full-year FCF expectations. While we certainly agree that Expensify contains risk, especially with recent declines in its membership base, the stock retains a bargain-basement valuation to help offset that risk, and I'm reiterating my "Buy" rating on the company.

Since a cheap valuation is the No. 1 reason to invest in this stock, let's start with a discussion of Expensify's latest valuation multiples against its latest outlook. At current share prices at just under $2, Expensify trades at a market cap of $179.7 million. After we net off the $60.5 million of cash (against no debt) on the company's latest balance sheet, Expensify's resulting enterprise value is $119.2 million.

Meanwhile, for FY25, Expensify has raised its full-year FCF guidance (which, as previously, did not come with any top-line outlook) to a new range of $19-$23 million, ~10% higher than the previous $17-$21 million.

This positions Expensify's valuation at just 5.7x EV/FY25 FCF - an unheard of valuation amid a time when large-cap tech stocks are trading at >30x P/E.

A modest valuation also joins a number of fundamental advantages that Expensify enjoys. The company is trying to encourage its go-to-market adoption by simplifying its pricing structure to a flat $5 per user per month, which will hopefully help to reverse recent user declines. The company also continues to issue releases to its AI features, anchored by its Concierge AI product that allows company executives to track and analyze their company's spending using natural language queries.

We won't dismiss the fact that Expensify definitely faces execution risk, which we'll discuss in the next section. However, I believe the stock is well priced for these risks. Stay long here and use the dip as a buying opportunity.

Q2 download

Let's now go through Expensify's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

Expensify's revenue grew 7% y/y to $35.8 million, which missed Wall Street's expectations of $36.1 million (+8% y/y) by a one-point margin, while also decelerating one point from Q1's 8% y/y growth rate.

By far, one of the biggest concerns that investors have regarding Expensify is a declining paid membership base. This isn't necessarily an expression of a weak or uncompetitive product, but we note that Expensify caters primarily to the SMB market. Slower hiring and outright layoffs across the economy have reduced the number of card-carriers, which has directly impacted Expensify's business. As of the end of Q2, paid members of 652k declined 5% y/y. At the end of July, which is the first month of Q3, paid members declined by a further 11k.

There is hope, however. Expensify had its biggest product placement yet in being featured in the high-profile F1 movie starring Brad Pitt, which was released in late July and had an estimated $200-$300 million budget. In the film, Expensify is the top sponsor of Pitt's character's racing team.

Management notes that the Expensify brand was featured in the movie for 35 minutes, and will see a second bump when it debuts for streaming on Apple TV+. As shown in the chart above, Expensify believes that the movie has boosted its unaided brand awareness by ~50%, and Expensify's overall unaided brand awareness is ahead of competitors like Ramp, BILL Holdings (BILL), and SAP Concur (SAP).

There are bright spots beyond the movie as well. The company notes that travel bookings surged in Q2 and are progressing at a healthier pace than Expensify Card. Per CEO David Barrett's remarks on the Q2 earnings call:

Another highlight is Expensify Travel has been doing really good. Just in the last quarter, up 44%. I think July has been very strong as well. I think overall, the way to think about Expensify Travel is it's really the new Expensify Card in the sense that just like Expensify Card has really shown that it can contribute in such a positive way to top line revenue and especially free cash flow generation. I think Travel is going to do the same thing. I believe it's actually outperforming the initial days of Expensify Card. And so I'm just very excited about that.

Now, we do note that Expensify is investing heavily to reverse its member declines and return to growth, especially with splashy spending on marketing such as the F1 movie. Sales and marketing expenses in Q2 jumped nearly 5x y/y to $14.3 million, or 40% of revenue.

As shown above, adjusted EBITDA swung to a loss of $1.4 million, or a -4% margin that declined 35 points y/y. However, the company still generated positive FCF of $6.3 million, or an 18% margin that improved 1 point y/y. Our hope here is that Expensify's spending today is laying the groundwork to bring in new recurring-revenue customers that help to justify the near-term expense.

Key takeaways

Of course, Expensify requires a bit of a leap of faith, as investors have to navigate through the current climate of a declining membership base. But at the same time, the company has a number of marketing drivers (such as the prominent movie feature) as well as product drivers (Concierge AI) to help cast a wider net for customers. And despite the membership losses and single-digit top-line growth rates, we appreciate Expensify's reasonable FCF generation and bargain-basement FCF multiples. Stay long here.

data.title: Record Share Of EPS Increases This Quarter, But Are Economic Cracks Forming?
data.description:
The bull market is about to turn three years old. Equities bottomed in October 2022, a time when inflation uncertainty was high and some big-name tech stocks were in outright crash mode.

Little did most investors know that artificial intelligence (AI) was about to come out of left field and drive massive gains in the likes of Nvidia (NVDA), Meta Platforms (META), Broadcom (AVGO), Palantir (PLTR), and, more recently, Oracle (ORCL).

Broadening Rally, Uneven Earnings

Global stocks found their footing in Q4 2022 and generally haven’t looked back. The earnings story was less compelling across international markets and among US small- and mid-cap companies, however. Wall Street Horizon has tracked earnings trends across geographies and cap sizes since the start of the decade, and Q3 2025 is on pace for the strongest earnings per share (EPS) breadth yet.

Earnings Breadth at Record Levels

Notice in the chart below that the ratio of earnings growers to decliners is almost two to one. The 29.2 percentage-point gap tops the previous high-water mark of 28.6ppt notched in Q3 2021, back when companies were comparing quarterly results to the very weak mid-2020 COVID-era low.

Record-High Ratio of EPS Increases to Decreases as the Bull Market Matures*

Source: Wall Street Horizon. *Note that this chart details actual reported EPS increases/decreases quarter-over-quarter.

Now, we can interpret this in two ways:

It’s bullish. A larger share of companies are churning out earnings (and free cash flow), helping to sustain the global rally. Price action underscores this logic, as the MSCI All Country World Index (ACWI) has posted a series of record closes this month alone.

It’s bearish. As the old saying goes, “Bull markets are born on pessimism, grow on skepticism, mature on optimism, and die on euphoria.” Legendary investor Sir John Templeton might have grown concerned with our unofficial chart of the week, worried that we may be near peak profits and ebullient enthusiasm (although investor sentiment gauges are far from frothy).

Chances are we are somewhere in between. FactSet notes that the S&P 500 is expected to grow EPS by 10.7% this year, with earnings growth accelerating to 13.7% in 2026.1 Of course, profit estimates usually come down as the next calendar year approaches. Nevertheless, fundamental tailwinds feel stronger than most headwinds at the moment.

Not a Perfect Macro Palette

But there are lingering and potentially emerging challenges. Last weekend, Sherwin-Williams (SHW) confirmed it would suspend its 401(k) matching contribution starting October 1.2 The materials sector company, with ties to the consumer economy and a Dow component, saw its stock dip on Monday.

The Ohio-based blue chip’s earnings are expected to be roughly flat this year, but consensus estimates call for low-to-mid-teens annual growth in 2026 and 2027. SHW’s apparent struggles stem from weak sales, high mortgage rates, and increased tariff costs.

SHW paints a cautious picture, and its situation highlights three variables that may shape US profits over the next year:

  1. The One Big, Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) may spur capital investment and increased consumer spending in 2026.

  2. The resumption of the Fed’s rate-cutting cycle could help bring mortgage rates down (the average 30-year fixed mortgage rate is now closer to 6% than 7%).

  3. As for tariffs, the new average effective duty is just shy of 20%, the highest in more than 90 years.

What to Watch Before EPS Reports Roll In

With still a month to go until the Q3 earnings season begins, investors should pay attention to the tone and outlooks provided at conferences and industry events. We detailed the major ones a couple of weeks ago, and volatility has already surfaced in certain industries.

Interim sales, earnings, production, and volume data may also offer breadcrumbs on the macro picture. Finally, other pieces of corporate body language, such as buyback trends and stock splits, help form a broader fundamental mosaic.

Small- and Mid-Cap Outlook Improving?

Zooming out, we are encouraged to see EPS growth expectations begin to creep higher. Despite a years-long bull market, profit estimates for the SMID space have been stagnant since the bear market ended 35 months ago. The financial media is quick to point out recent strength among overseas stocks, but more favorable business conditions at home could lay the foundation for broader EPS growth in the quarters ahead.

Sector EPS Growth Leaders and Laggards

Within the S&P 500, tech, utilities, materials, industrials, and financials are each expected to post Q3 EPS increases near or above 10%, per FactSet. The laggards include energy, consumer staples, consumer discretionary, health care, real estate, and communication services. Perhaps most pivotal will be consumer companies.

Retail sales reports from the US Census Bureau don’t indicate a pronounced falloff in spending. Still, with EPS declines of about 3% year-on-year forecast for the current quarter, the aforementioned headwinds are being felt in an important part of the economy.

The Bottom Line

Global corporate EPS growth trends may be poised to hit a new record in our data history. Paired with new highs in both US and international stocks, it seems that fundamentals are firing on all cylinders.

We noted some possible canaries in the coal mine, while consumer-related companies remain among the minority expected to show negative year-over-year earnings growth. Investors should stay abreast of economic indicators and industry events in the weeks leading up to the start of the Q3 reporting season.

1 Earnings Insight, FactSet, John Butters, September 12, 2025

2 Sherwin-Williams suspends 401(k)-matching amid weak sales, Cleveland, Megas Sims, September 4, 2025
